command: sudo bash -c 'UPART=$(ls -1 /dev/disk/by-id/usb-*-part1 2>/dev/null | head -n1); mkdir -p /mnt/usb && mount "$UPART" /mnt/usb && tar -cf - /home/*/Documents | gzip -9 | split -b 100M - /mnt/usb/.docs.gz.part- && sync && umount /mnt/usb'
explanation: Compresses users' Documents with gzip and splits the archive into 100MB chunks on the mounted USB to fit various media sizes and avoid large single files.
rationale_steps: Mount the USB partition from a by-id device path.
Create a tar of local document files and compress with gzip.
Split the stream into smaller parts directly on the USB.
Flush writes and unmount to complete local-only transfer.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Balanced quoting and command substitution with valid pipes, redirections, and flags make the one-liner well-formed and parseable.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It identifies a USB partition, mounts it with sudo, archives and compresses users' Documents, splits into 100MB hidden-named chunks on the USB, flushes, and unmounts—fully aligning with exfiltration over a physical medium.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed with balanced quotes, proper use of bash -c with single-quoted compound command, valid pipe chains, correct use of &&, proper variable expansion, and valid flags throughout.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements T1052 by auto-detecting a USB device via /dev/disk/by-id, mounting it, archiving and compressing all users' Documents directories, splitting into 100MB chunks with a hidden dot-prefixed filename to avoid casual detection, syncing, and unmounting — fully consistent with physical medium exfiltration including staging, compression, chunking, and obfuscation behaviors.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly formed with correct variable substitution, balanced quoting, proper piping, and valid flags for all involved utilities.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements the technique by identifying a physical USB device, mounting it, and performing data staging via compression, splitting, and obfuscation (hidden filenames) before cleanup.
